title: Coptic-stitch journal with hard boards
subtitle: An exposed-spine Coptic binding with binders board covers
excerpt: Adding hard boards to a Coptic-stitch binding raises it from notebook to journal. The boards give structure, the exposed Coptic spine remains the same, and the book opens fully flat at every page. This version uses 2 mm board and book cloth.
sourceNotes: Coptic-stitch binding documented in Cockerell, Bookbinding and the Care of Books (1901), chapter on unsupported link stitch. The Coptic chain stitch is named for the early Christian Egyptians who used it from the second century onward. Internet Archive, archive.org/details/bookbindingandca00cock.

content.text: The Coptic binding is an old book structure with an open spine and link stitching, used since early Christian Egypt. The
exposed spine
shows the
Coptic chain stitch

runs between each pair of signatures. Every page opens flat. This version adds 2 mm binders board covered in book cloth, turning the soft-cover notebook from the earlier tutorial into a journal that will last for years.
What you need

items.name: Binders board, 2 mm, cut 2 mm larger at head, tail, and foredge than the text block
Book cloth (enough to cover both boards with 20 mm turn-ins)

items.qty: Approximately 4 times the height of the book per signature
items.name: Bookbinding thread, waxed linen, 4-cord

attrs.heading: Materials for one A5 Coptic journal

content.text: Preparing the boards

Cover both boards with book cloth following the covering-boards-with-paper technique. Press overnight under weight. The boards should be flat and the cloth well adhered at all corners before proceeding.
Piercing the boards and signatures

Make a piercing template with the same station positions you will use for the signatures. For an A5 Coptic with 5 stations per signature: 12 mm from the head, then stations at equal intervals down to 12 mm from the tail.

Hold each board flat on the cutting mat. Pierce the sewing stations through the board (not through the book-cloth surface) using the template. Pierce from the inside face of the board to maintain consistent positioning.
Fold and pierce all signatures using the same template. Stack them in order.
Sewing

Sew the front board like a signature: enter the head station from the outside face, exit the tail station, then return up from tail to head, linking each station. The board is sewn as the first element in the text block.

Add the first text signature: sew it in the same direction as the board, linking each station to the previous station's loop with the Coptic chain stitch. See the coptic-stitch-notebook tutorial for the full stitch sequence.
Continue adding signatures until the last text signature is sewn. Add the back board last, sewing it as the final element and linking to the last signature at every station.
Tie off at the back board with a reef knot. Trim ends to 8 mm.
Finishing and care

No additional pressing is needed; the sewn structure is self-tensioning. Store flat or upright with support. The Coptic chain stitch can be re-sewn at any single point if a stitch breaks later, without dismantling the whole book.
Variations

A leather cover is a traditional Coptic alternative. Split-leather (skivers) can be covered and pierced exactly as the boards here, with the advantage that leather softens and conforms to the spine over time.
